Support Committee whoa to this agenda.
Karen. Numr of staff members with us here today as well in particular want to call out Professor Bob Buckle
who's term on the NPC finishes after this meeting. Bob was one of our originals back in April 2019,
so I said for six and a half years, just want to acknowledge his contribution.
So you would have seen yesterday the Monetary Policy Committee voted
to reduce the cash rate by 25 basis points to 3%. While consumer price inflation is
heading towards the top of our 1% to 3% target range, there is significant spare capacity
in the economy, which gives us confidence that headline inflation will be heading back
to the midpoint of our target by the middle of next year. The main news since the May report
when we last met with you is that New Zealand's economic recovery stalled
in the second quarter of this year. Spending by households and businesses has been constrained
by heightened global policy uncertainty, falling employment, higher prices for some essentials
and declining house prices. Looking forward, we see upside and downside risks to the outlook.
Cautious behaviour by households and businesses could further dampen growth.
Alternatively, the economic recovery could accelerate as the full effects of the interest
rate cuts so far.
a start to flow through the economy.
Further data on the speed of New Zealand's economic recovery will determine our next steps
from here.
That said, if inflation pressures over the medium turn continue to ease as expected,
we see scope to continue lowering the official cash rate.
Chair, I will finish there and open it up for questions.
OK, we've got a Don, Deborah Russell-Labor.
OK, thank you, always interesting.
Governor, I've been looking at the appendices and particularly the summary of economic projections
table 6.5 and comparing the August MPS to the figures that you had in the May MPS
in terms of those economic projections.
And if I look in particular at the figures around output and around GDP,
they're all kind of the projections in August or somewhat more pessimistic than the projections in May.
So you've revised downwards if it were, and if I look at the now cast as well,
I love your now cast, so it's tracking below zero from about June onwards
and still below zero in August.
So, I'm puzzled because people keep on talking about green shoots in the economy
and that perhaps a September quarter will be better, but the data you've got here suggests not.
Can you just expand on that a little bit?
So the big news since May has been our revision of GDP for Q2 this year.
So that's the months of April, May and June.
Last time we were here, we had a projection for that quarter of plus 0.3%.
So we were thinking that the economy would keep growing through that period.
We've revised down that projection to...
negatif 0.3%.
We're seeing our assessment
as the economy contracted through those three months.
That's really what's driven our change in policy stance
over that period with a lower OCR track
to respond to that more spare capacity in the economy.
That Q3 number then flows through into those numbers
that you were looking at in terms of the implication
of how it's measured and your growth
through the calendar year and further out.
So, I'm struggling to see these green shirts.
So what we're hearing is that the agricultural sector
is doing quite well, but instead of necessarily spending up more,
they are tending to blow that into debt reduction.
So how are we going to get anything going
if we've got this huge under-utilisation?
There seems to be nothing doing
that's actually going to stimulate growth in the economy.
So that's where the lags and monetary policy come in.
We've been cutting interest rates quite aggressively
for a little over 12 months.
Now, we know that it takes 12 to 18 months
for the full effects of any interest rate changes
to flow through the economy, through activity,
and into ultimately inflation.
What we've seen is the reaction to our interest rate cuts
so far being a bit slower than we had anticipated,
and that's partly because things change in the interim.
We had Liberation Day in early April
and all the global policy uncertainty
that's been surrounded by that.
The surprise for us since May has been
what a prolonged impact that's had
on business and consumer confidence in New Zealand,
despite the fact that we are kind of outside of those...
worst case scenarios that we talked about in May around how that could have escalated and
got much, much worse. So there is a bit of a hang over there. We are seeing the July numbers
that are starting to come through are starting to pick up. So we are seeing the PMI and
the PSI that were out over the last week or so are looking more in positive space.
We've got interest rates, effective interest rates for mortgage holders that will keep
rolling lower as they come off those fixed rates and refixed at lower interest
rates. We just think monetary policy eventually works and we start getting
that recovery. The other thing that's happened is the uncertainty measures
have come off as well. So at the peak of ongoing discussions globally around
what was actually going to happen with tariffs, uncertainty measures just
escalated dramatically and they've come right off as well. So there's a feeling
of more certainty around understanding where tariffs may land and the
retaliatory issues that everybody was wondering about whether they would
happen, have not happened. So as a consequence of that, there is a
higher degree of certainty that's sitting there. Not great. It's not good
for anybody that tariffs exist and that they are heightened, but at least so.
And whilst New Zealand's up, 15 versus 10, on average, those numbers that have
come out to date are definitely aligned with what our expectations were in
May. So just sort of, I appreciate, I won't just sort of set the tariff
aside. I'm sure other people will bring it up and say, but I just know that
yesterday Paul, you said that the RBNZ can only do so much to help stimulate
growth and productivity. So can you

Just to suck just on the leg that you talked about
in terms of the flow on for interest rates
and noticing that there was a split decision of the committee.
Really interesting some of the debate that we've torn around.
0.5 increase because on page 34 of the report you talk about that some households haven't taken
advantage of fixing the lowest rates because they're anticipating further rate cuts and was
thinking you've signalled two more rate cuts is the danger that people are going to delay
realising the full potential of falling interest rates because they're still waiting out
to the end of the year for the lowest rates and what was the thinking of the bank
and the debate that went on at the committee? I'll pass to Karen in a minute on the sort of
affected mortgage rates and the like, but just to speak firstly to the vote and the different
that were around the committee. The first one was consensus around the weaker economic activity
that occurred and therefore the amount of more spare capacity there in the economy and
that central projection that interest rates could go lower over the projection period so we were
aligned around that. Then it was really the argument for a 50 basis point cut was that
businesses and consumers were being overly cautious and they really needed quite a large
substantial kick to sort of get out of that rut and lift that confidence and have that sort
of guardrail that monetary policy is here to support the economic recovery. That was the argument
for 50. Was that bringing forward one of the signalled later cuts? Yes, it was an issue of
timing effectively. That's a very, very good point. So those voting for, we were all the same on
the path. Those voting for 50, it was about timing. Bring it forward, get it in early.
The argument for the 25 was actually we've done a lot already.

I think we use it in a reasonably plain fashion in the sense of GDP growth.
OK, thank you. So, to that effect, we've currently got a situation which you yourself
and your thoughts speak to around oil wage inflation has slowed sharply over the last year
and at the same time inflation around some essential expenditure components such as
food, gas, electricity and council rates have been much higher than the general rate
of inflation. You obviously also alluded to how government investment has decreased
in 2025 contributing to the slow down in economic activity. You well know that
I'm very concerned about distributional impacts in our economy. Monetary policy
is obviously a blunt instrument. I guess the point that I'm trying to get to
is fiscal policy is slowing down the economy right now. It is retreating.
That is the only policy which can redistribute kind of material impacts.
Thank you.

So, what matters for us is what's happening in our projection horizon, and we use the budget numbers from May, which would show that total government spending as a proportion of output in the economy is falling over that period.

And that's something that sort of reduces inflation pressures, and so that's how it fits in for us. Within that aggregate number, that's where the story about redistribution and the more surgical ability of fiscal and government policy relative to monetary policy comes in.

So, there are different choices for governments around how they, you know, how they do everything under the bonnet there from where that spending goes, how it's used, their policies around promoting growth, trying to promote new skills, new technology, new ways of working, new products, all of those things.
So, all of that happens under those aggregate numbers. Paul, do you want to add anything?

Well, I think you covered it extremely well, Governor. For me, Chloe, just like recovery is, like I think of it in terms of closing that output gap. So, we've had like demand significantly below the supply capacity of this economy for some time.

And a recovery is, for me, at least to see that close. In terms of inequality, like I don't think that growth is not necessarily good, or it doesn't necessarily impact on inequality one way or the other.
So, you can have, you know,

well-distributed across people. You can, or you can have growth accru into a small number of people and a technology shock, for example, if people like AI for example, if it's only a small group of people that sort of own that capital, and if that does sort of do what seems to be happening, potentially at least, then that group of people will enrich themselves and not any better.
that group of people will enrich themselves and the last thing I'll say you know inequality is not
like as you know monetary policy is a blunt tool so it's other levers of government that influence
inequality and yes on the fiscal side but also on the tax side and regulatory settings and all
of that you know I think in this economy monetary policy you know a lot gets put on us
you know and we turn up every kind of six weeks and we do our thing and we get tons of media
about it which is fine but it's about the business cycle and it's about inflation it's not about
those long-run structural features of the New Zealand economy and it's and it's not about
inequality you know we don't have the ability to influence those things as much as as as government
and other arms of the public yeah well I guess I really I really do appreciate that point and
that is precisely what it is that we're trying to sketch is what you actually have control over
and what is the responsibility of government to that effect noting what can and can't happen or
what may or may not happen with regards to tracks for growth and you know if you're saying that
recovery looks like GDP going back up are you guys tracking based on all of the current
variables that you're aware of whether that recovery that you are saying that we're looking
out for is that going to be relatively equally felt across the economy or is it going to be unequal
i.e. those lower socioeconomic households going to be paying the cost for this

We acknowledge at the moment that there are parts of society who haven't felt like there's been low and stable inflation at the moment.
I'm not blaming you guys for this.
I'm just trying to say of the variables that you are tracking and of the economic recovery that you are forecasting,
is it going to be relatively equally felt across?
I'm not saying that you have responsibility for this.
I'm just asking in what you're tracking.
Do you want to even go that one?
Because we don't have a big impact on inequality,
but we need to understand it because it affects how monetary policy transmits through the economy.
For example, if lower income people get a tax cut, for example,
it's more likely to be spent rather than saved.
So that affects how tax policy transmits through the economy.
So we keep an eye on it, but we don't track it.
In terms of whether this growth is going to push inequality in one way or the other,
the fact that house prices have been a source of increased wealth inequality in New Zealand
over the decades and the fact that we're not seeing much of that happen,
or we're not projecting much of that to happen over the coming quarters
when we are getting reasonable growth by the end of this year,
that's sort of a partial answer on the wealth side.
I've got less to say on the income.
OK, thank you very much.

I wanted to come back to the question on global trade.
beti awaiai. So, your statement projects the 0.8% rise in the tradeable goods and then

tradeable inflation tends to be quite volatile. So, we need to be careful about responding to that
too quickly. It's really more about whether it gets embedded into inflation expectations and
sort of pushes up those medium-term pressures. When it comes to the global outlook and the
tariff situation, the issue there is, as I mentioned earlier, back in May it was unclear
how much that would escalate. It hasn't escalated as much as we feared. So, in that sense, we are in a
better position globally. We do continue to see

for the economy and really creating some slack in the economy to bring domestic inflation pressures down so what we're seeing at the moment is the result of that calling off period in the labour market and as you say it's coming through all those different indicators that you see there

what we're saying is that we think we've got inflation under control now and that puts us in a position where we think we're at the tail end of that process that we had gone through and from here we're seeing economic recovery pick up we've got GDP growth numbers picking up strongly into the end of this year and into the beginning of next year

and we think that's what will drive an improvement in the labour market in terms of those blues are going to turn back into the greens and oranges on that dashboard as we come through the other end of this cycle.

Essential prices for essentials.
Absolutely. But one of the things that I found interesting in that answer is in terms of your mandate. So, you've of course, the government changed the bank's mandate to focus solely on inflation.
Has that changed the way in which the bank is making decisions in terms of that key thing for making things better for people?
And that's the ability to be in work or to access the labour market. I mean, what we're seeing in the report is a lot of people just giving up.

So, it's not just the unemployment rate. It's the underemployment rate. People that are removing themselves from even consideration in the labour market into what extent has the government removing your ability to look at employment as a factor in setting your rates
had an impact.
Okay, so we'll answer that and then we're going to go to Ryan.

So, thank you. So, first thing is, the Reserve Bank has always cared about the labour market. And we've always monitored and focused on the labour market because it's a key indicator of not only of the state of that part of the economy, but the implications that has for inflation as well, when the labour market's overheating or the labour market's underheating that.

you've signalled quite well, and I've sort of noticed for the first time, Page 42,
how you've actually gone out a couple of years in four-
to the OCR, which is quite useful in terms of giving some certainty of direction.
Why not be a little bit more aggressive than, obviously, it was a robust conversation
and just drop it in a 50 basis points now when you know there's going to be this lag?
Why the sort of overly cautious approach?
Kind of goes back to that discussion we had earlier around the question not being about
how much stimulus to provide in total, in terms of that projection, but more timing.
So those in favour of a 50 basis point cut would bring it forward, get more of a kick
straight away, have more of a surprise and more of a guardrail from the Reserve Bank.
Those in favour of 25 were acknowledged that actually the work we've done so far
is still working its way through, as Karen described earlier, but also that signalling
benefit from the fact that what matters is not just how we change the OCR on the day,
but it's the projection, it's the fact that we had a vote, all of those things
in totality will influence how much we can move the market.
And so what we saw yesterday was actually market rates fallen quite significantly,
and despite it only being 25 and 25 being expected, it was all this other stuff that
wasn't expected, that helped us move rates. The challenge you have with a 50 is
it's the communication around are you providing more stimulus or are you just saying we're
going to do more now and do less later? There is a challenge there that markets can get confused
that they think, oh well, they're panicking, they're going to do more,
that they don't really often don't get it, that you can just be about timing.
No, thank you, and I guess finally you report.
